meta.title: SF-SPAA-1: Account Setup, Resource Monitors and Network Policies

questions.stem: A company wants to follow the principle of least privilege for daily Snowflake administration tasks. Which system role should be used as the primary role for creating and managing database objects, while ensuring ACCOUNTADMIN is reserved for account-level operations only?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN, because it has all privileges and can do everything needed
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN should be reserved for account-level operations; using it for daily tasks violates least privilege.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN, because it can manage all roles and users
options.explanation: is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN is focused on role and user management (GRANT/REVOKE privileges, CREATE USER), not object creation.

options.text: SYSADMIN, because it is designed to create and manage objects without full account-level access
options.explanation: is correct. SYSADMIN is specifically designed for creating and owning database objects (databases, schemas, tables, warehouses) and should be used for day-to-day object management. ACCOUNTADMIN has all privileges including billing and account management — best practice is to use it sparingly and only for tasks that require it.

options.text: PUBLIC, because it is automatically granted to all users
options.explanation: is incorrect. PUBLIC is granted to every user automatically and is not appropriate for administrative object creation.

questions.stem: An organization creates a custom role hierarchy for their Snowflake environment. A new custom role ANALYST_ROLE is created but NOT granted to SYSADMIN. What is the consequence of this omission?

options.text: Users with ANALYST_ROLE cannot log into Snowflake
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no login restriction from this omission; users can still authenticate and use the role.

options.text: SYSADMIN cannot manage or drop objects owned by ANALYST_ROLE, and ACCOUNTADMIN is required to administer those objects
options.explanation: is correct. When a custom role is not granted to SYSADMIN (directly or through a chain), SYSADMIN cannot manage objects owned by that role, because Snowflake's access control requires a role to have the OWNERSHIP privilege (or inherit it) to manage objects. Only ACCOUNTADMIN (which inherits from all roles) or the role itself can then manage those objects. Best practice is to always grant custom roles to SYSADMIN so object management is centralized.

options.text: ANALYST_ROLE cannot be granted any privileges on tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. ANALYST_ROLE can still be granted privileges on tables regardless of whether it is in the SYSADMIN hierarchy.

options.text: The role will automatically be deleted after 30 days
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not automatically delete roles based on time.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to set a maximum statement execution time of 2 hours (7200 seconds) for all sessions in the account, except for a specific warehouse used by data engineering pipelines which should have no timeout. Which combination of commands accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 0;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200 sets the account-wide default. Setting the same parameter to 0 on the specific warehouse overrides the account-level setting for that warehouse, effectively disabling the timeout for queries running on it.

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 0;
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOCK_TIMEOUT controls how long a query waits for a lock, not overall statement execution time.

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS = 7200; DROP PARAMETER STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS ON WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh;
options.explanation: is incorrect. The DROP PARAMETER syntax is not valid for this purpose; the correct approach is to SET the parameter to 0 at the warehouse level.

options.text: CREATE SESSION POLICY account_timeout SESSION_TIMEOUT = 7200; ALTER WAREHOUSE pipeline_wh UNSET SESSION_POLICY;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SESSION POLICY controls session idle timeout, not statement execution time.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is creating a password policy to enforce corporate security standards. Select TWO requirements that can be enforced using a Snowflake PASSWORD POLICY object. (Select TWO)

options.text: Minimum password length of 14 characters
options.explanation: is correct. PASSWORD POLICY supports minimum length via the PASSWORD_MIN_LENGTH parameter.

options.text: Password must not match any of the last 10 passwords used
options.explanation: is correct. PASSWORD_HISTORY prevents reuse of recent passwords, so the last N passwords cannot be reused.

options.text: Password expires automatically when the user has not logged in for 90 days
options.explanation: is incorrect. Password expiry based on inactivity is controlled by LOGIN POLICY or account-level settings, not PASSWORD POLICY.

options.text: Automatic account lockout after 5 failed login attempts
options.explanation: is incorrect. Account lockout after failed attempts is managed through LOGIN POLICY (MAX_LOGIN_FAILURES), not PASSWORD POLICY.

questions.stem: A company has the following role hierarchy: SYSADMIN -> DATA_OPS_ROLE -> ETL_ROLE. The ETL_ROLE owns a table SALES.PUBLIC.ORDERS. A user with only the DATA_OPS_ROLE active tries to drop the table. What happens?

options.text: The drop succeeds because DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits OWNERSHIP from ETL_ROLE through the hierarchy
options.explanation: is correct. In Snowflake's role hierarchy, privileges flow UPWARD — from granted roles to the roles that contain them. Because DATA_OPS_ROLE has ETL_ROLE granted to it, DATA_OPS_ROLE inherits all privileges held by ETL_ROLE, including OWNERSHIP of the ORDERS table. Therefore the drop succeeds.

options.text: The drop fails because privilege inheritance flows upward (from child to parent), so DATA_OPS_ROLE does not inherit ETL_ROLE's OWNERSHIP privilege
options.explanation: is incorrect. This reverses the direction of privilege inheritance. A parent role DOES inherit privileges from child roles granted to it.

options.text: The drop succeeds because SYSADMIN has OWNERSHIP over all objects
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN also inherits the OWNERSHIP transitively, but that is not the reason the drop succeeds — DATA_OPS_ROLE itself has the inherited privilege.

options.text: The drop fails because only ACCOUNTADMIN can drop tables
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN is not required to drop tables; any role with OWNERSHIP can drop the object.

questions.stem: An administrator receives a request to create a new user for an application service account with a 90-day key pair authentication setup and no interactive login capability. Which command correctly creates this user?

options.text: CREATE USER svc_app TYPE = SERVICE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE DISABLED = FALSE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = SERVICE is valid in newer Snowflake versions but alone does not guarantee no interactive login without also disabling password authentication.

options.text: CREATE USER svc_app PASSWORD = NULL RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = FALSE;
options.explanation: is correct. Setting PASSWORD = NULL disables password-based login for the service account while RSA_PUBLIC_KEY enables key pair authentication. MUST_CHANGE_PASSWORD = FALSE ensures the account does not prompt for a password change on first login.

options.text: CREATE USER svc_app TYPE = SERVICE LOGIN_NAME = NULL RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>';
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = SERVICE with LOGIN_NAME = NULL is not valid syntax for disabling interactive login.

options.text: CREATE USER svc_app NO_PASSWORD RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<public_key>' DEFAULT_ROLE = APP_ROLE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. NO_PASSWORD is not a valid Snowflake CREATE USER parameter.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to implement a session policy to automatically log out idle users after 30 minutes. Select TWO statements that are true about Snowflake session policies. (Select TWO)

options.text: A session policy can be set at the account level using ALTER ACCOUNT SET SESSION_POLICY = policy_name
options.explanation: is correct. A session policy can be applied at the account level using ALTER ACCOUNT SET SESSION_POLICY and at the user level using ALTER USER SET SESSION_POLICY.

options.text: A session policy set on a specific user overrides the account-level session policy for that user
options.explanation: is correct. User-level session policies take precedence over account-level policies for that specific user.

options.text: SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS in a session policy controls both the web UI and programmatic client idle timeouts equally
options.explanation: is incorrect. SESSION_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MINS applies primarily to web UI sessions; programmatic clients (JDBC, ODBC, connectors) have separate timeout behavior and are not identically governed by this parameter alone.

options.text: Session policies can enforce a maximum session duration regardless of activity
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake session policies control idle timeout, not absolute maximum session duration.

questions.stem: Which system role is specifically designed to create and manage users and roles, but does NOT have the ability to modify account-level parameters or manage billing?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN has all privileges including billing and account parameters — it is the most powerful role.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSADMIN creates and manages database objects, not users and roles.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. SECURITYADMIN can manage all aspects of access control including granting/revoking any privilege; it inherits USERADMIN and has broader scope.

options.text: USERADMIN
options.explanation: is correct. USERADMIN is specifically scoped to creating and managing users and roles (CREATE USER, ALTER USER, CREATE ROLE). It does not have account-level parameter access or billing visibility.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to be notified when credit consumption on a specific warehouse reaches 75% of its monthly quota, and have all running queries cancelled immediately when it reaches 100%. Which resource monitor trigger configuration achieves this?

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO ALERT ON 100 PERCENT DO CANCEL_ALL
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALERT and CANCEL_ALL are not valid resource monitor trigger actions in Snowflake.

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND
options.explanation: is incorrect. SUSPEND (not SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY) lets running queries finish before suspending — it does not immediately cancel them.

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO NOTIFY ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
options.explanation: is correct. NOTIFY sends an email alert without affecting warehouse operation. SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY cancels all currently running queries on the warehouse and prevents new ones from starting.

options.text: TRIGGERS ON 75 PERCENT DO SUSPEND ON 100 PERCENT DO SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY
options.explanation: is incorrect. This skips the notification phase at 75%, and SUSPEND at 75% would prematurely halt the warehouse rather than just notify.

questions.stem: An organization's ACCOUNTADMIN notices that total Snowflake credit consumption is unexpectedly high this month. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides a breakdown of credit consumption by service type (compute, serverless, cloud services) on a daily basis?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY is at the warehouse level by hour and does not include serverless credits.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. METERING_HISTORY provides hourly warehouse-level data similar to WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY and does not break out all service types.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily aggregated credit consumption broken down by service type (virtual warehouses, cloud services, serverless features like Snowpipe and automatic clustering). It is the best view for identifying which service type is driving unexpected costs.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
options.explanation: is incorrect. STORAGE_USAGE tracks bytes stored, not compute or serverless credits.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a resource monitor at the account level and assigns it to two specific warehouses. Select TWO true statements about this configuration. (Select TWO)

options.text: Credits consumed by both warehouses count toward the same shared quota on the account-level resource monitor
options.explanation: is incorrect. When warehouses have their own warehouse-level monitors, credits consumed do not count against the account-level monitor quota.

options.text: Credits consumed by serverless features (e.g., Snowpipe) also count toward the resource monitor quota
options.explanation: is incorrect. Serverless feature credits (Snowpipe, automatic clustering) are NOT tracked by resource monitors — they require the Budget feature for granular control.

options.text: A warehouse-level resource monitor on one of those warehouses takes precedence over the account-level resource monitor for that warehouse
options.explanation: is correct. When a resource monitor is assigned to a specific warehouse, it takes precedence over the account-level resource monitor for that warehouse. The account-level monitor then only applies to warehouses without their own assigned monitor.

options.text: The resource monitor quota resets at the start of each calendar month by default
options.explanation: is correct. Resource monitor quotas reset monthly by default (FREQUENCY = MONTHLY).

questions.stem: A company uses automatic clustering on several large tables. The finance team reports unexpectedly high credit costs but no corresponding large warehouse usage. Where should the administrator look to identify the source of these serverless credits?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY filtered by warehouse type = 'STANDARD'
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY does not capture serverless credits — automatic clustering does not use virtual warehouses.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY
options.explanation: is a plausible alternative. AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY also exists and provides table-level detail, but METERING_DAILY_HISTORY is the better cross-service aggregate view for identifying the source of unexpectedly high costs.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY filtered by SERVICE_TYPE = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING'
options.explanation: is correct. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY includes a SERVICE_TYPE column that distinguishes between WAREHOUSE_METERING, AUTO_CLUSTERING, SNOWPIPE, and other service types. Filtering by SERVICE_TYPE = 'AUTO_CLUSTERING' isolates those costs and provides daily totals across the account.

options.text: SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and check the USED_CREDITS column
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS does not track serverless credits; resource monitors only track virtual warehouse credits.

questions.stem: A resource monitor with CREDIT_QUOTA = 500 and FREQUENCY = WEEKLY is assigned to a warehouse. On Wednesday, the warehouse has consumed 480 credits. The trigger is set to SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY at 100%. A long-running query is executing when the 500-credit threshold is hit. What happens to that query?

options.text: The query completes normally because it was already running before the threshold was hit
options.explanation: is incorrect. Unlike SUSPEND (which allows running queries to finish), SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY does not grant in-flight queries any grace.

options.text: The query is queued and will run when the weekly quota resets
options.explanation: is incorrect. Queries are not queued for the next period; they must be re-submitted manually after the warehouse is resumed.

options.text: The query is immediately cancelled and the warehouse is suspended
options.explanation: is correct. SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY is the most aggressive trigger action — it cancels ALL queries currently running on the warehouse and immediately suspends it, regardless of whether they were in progress when the threshold was crossed.

options.text: The query runs for an additional grace period of 1 hour before being cancelled
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no grace period with SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY.

questions.stem: A data analyst complains they cannot query a table in the SALES schema even though they have SELECT privileges on the table. After investigation, the administrator finds the analyst's role has no USAGE privilege on the SALES schema. Which statement fixes this without granting unnecessary additional privileges?

options.text: GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT ALL is excessive and violates the least privilege principle.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT SELECT on tables is already present per the scenario — re-granting it does not solve the missing schema USAGE.

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA SALES TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
options.explanation: is correct. To access objects within a schema, a role must have USAGE privilege on the schema. GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA is the targeted, least-privilege fix for the stated problem.

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE SALES_DB TO ROLE ANALYST_ROLE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE addresses a different missing privilege; it is necessary but not sufficient on its own, and the problem identified is the schema-level privilege.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator creates a schema using CREATE SCHEMA analytics WITH MANAGED ACCESS. A table REPORTS is created in this schema. Which role can grant SELECT on REPORTS to other roles?

options.text: The role that owns the REPORTS table (the object owner)
options.explanation: is incorrect. This is explicitly the opposite of managed access schema behavior — the whole point is to remove this ability from object owners.

options.text: Any role with USAGE on the analytics schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. Holding USAGE on the schema does not confer the ability to grant privileges on objects within it.

options.text: Only the schema owner or a role with the MANAGE GRANTS privilege (such as SECURITYADMIN)
options.explanation: is correct. In a managed access schema, privilege management is centralized — the object owner CANNOT grant privileges on their own objects to other roles. Only the schema owner (or a role with the MANAGE GRANTS privilege, such as SECURITYADMIN or ACCOUNTADMIN) can grant privileges on objects within a managed access schema.

options.text: Any role with the SELECT privilege on REPORTS and WITH GRANT OPTION
options.explanation: is incorrect. In a managed access schema, WITH GRANT OPTION does not apply in the usual way; privilege grants flow through the schema owner only.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to ensure that any new table created in the REPORTING schema automatically gets SELECT granted to the BI_ROLE. Select TWO commands that together accomplish this for both existing and future tables. (Select TWO)

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
options.explanation: is correct. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA covers existing tables at the moment of execution.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
options.explanation: is correct. GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA sets up automatic grants for any tables created in that schema going forward. Together A and B provide complete coverage.

options.text: ALTER SCHEMA REPORTING SET DEFAULT_GRANTS = SELECT TO ROLE BI_ROLE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER SCHEMA SET DEFAULT_GRANTS is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA REPORTING TO ROLE BI_ROLE WITH GRANT OPTION;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT USAGE WITH GRANT OPTION allows BI_ROLE to grant USAGE to others, which is unrelated to SELECT on tables and does not auto-grant SELECT.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to transfer ownership of a table from ROLE_A to ROLE_B, but wants to revoke all existing grants that ROLE_A made on that table. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B;
options.explanation: is incorrect. Without REVOKE or COPY, the default behavior depends on context, but REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS is the explicit form.

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B COPY CURRENT GRANTS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. COPY CURRENT GRANTS preserves all existing privileges — the opposite of what is required here.

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TO ROLE ROLE_B REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;
options.explanation: is correct. GRANT OWNERSHIP ... REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership to ROLE_B and simultaneously revokes all access privileges that were previously granted on the table. This is the explicit, unambiguous syntax for a clean ownership transfer.

options.text: ALTER TABLE mydb.myschema.orders TRANSFER OWNERSHIP TO ROLE_B REVOKE ALL GRANTS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER TABLE TRANSFER OWNERSHIP is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem:
A security team requires that users from the SALES_TEAM role can only see rows in the CUSTOMERS table where the REGION column matches their own region assignment, stored in a mapping table. A row access policy is created as follows:

CREATE ROW ACCESS POLICY region_policy AS (region_col VARCHAR) RETURNS BOOLEAN ->
  EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM region_mapping WHERE user_region = region_col AND username = CURRENT_USER());

The policy is applied. A user with ACCOUNTADMIN role queries CUSTOMERS. What rows does ACCOUNTADMIN see?

options.text: All rows, because row access policies do not apply to ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake row access policies do NOT have a special bypass for ACCOUNTADMIN — this is a critical distinction from some other systems.

options.text: All rows, because ACCOUNTADMIN automatically bypasses all security policies
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNTADMIN does not automatically bypass row access policies.

options.text: Only rows where the user's name appears in region_mapping, same as any other user
options.explanation: is correct. Row access policies in Snowflake apply to ALL roles, including ACCOUNTADMIN. The policy evaluates based on CURRENT_USER() against the region_mapping table. If ACCOUNTADMIN's username is in the mapping, they see matching rows; if not, they see no rows.

options.text: No rows, because ACCOUNTADMIN is not in the region_mapping table
options.explanation: is incorrect. This gets the outcome right (no rows if not in mapping) but misrepresents the mechanism — the correct reason is that the policy applies the same logic to all roles, and if ACCOUNTADMIN is absent from region_mapping, EXISTS returns false.

questions.stem: A company wants to restrict Snowflake access to only their corporate IP range (192.168.10.0/24) and explicitly block a known suspicious IP (203.0.113.50). Which command creates a network policy that achieves this?

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('192.168.10.0/24') BLOCKED_IP_LIST = ('203.0.113.50');
options.explanation: is correct. The correct Snowflake syntax uses ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST parameters. BLOCKED_IP_LIST takes precedence over ALLOWED_IP_LIST.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy WHITELIST = ('192.168.10.0/24') BLACKLIST = ('203.0.113.50');
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does not use WHITELIST/BLACKLIST terminology.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOW = ('192.168.10.0/24') DENY = ('203.0.113.50');
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALLOW/DENY are not valid Snowflake network policy parameters.

options.text: CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy IP_ALLOWLIST ('192.168.10.0/24') IP_BLOCKLIST ('203.0.113.50');
options.explanation: is incorrect. The parameters require an equals sign and the correct names are ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST.

questions.stem: A company has set a network policy at the account level restricting access to a corporate VPN IP range. An individual developer needs access from a personal IP that is outside the corporate range for an emergency. How can an administrator grant this without changing the account-level policy?

options.text: Temporarily disable the account-level network policy using ALTER ACCOUNT UNSET NETWORK_POLICY
options.explanation: is incorrect. Disabling the account policy removes protection for all users — a serious security risk.

options.text: Add the developer's IP to the account-level ALLOWED_IP_LIST temporarily
options.explanation: is incorrect. Modifying the account-level policy affects all users and is not a targeted solution.

options.text: Create a separate network policy that includes the developer's IP and assign it to that specific user with ALTER USER SET NETWORK_POLICY
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake supports network policies at both the account level and the user level. A user-level network policy overrides the account-level policy for that specific user. The administrator creates a new policy with the developer's IP and assigns it using ALTER USER developer_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = dev_policy.

options.text: Network policies cannot be scoped to individual users; the account-level policy must be modified
options.explanation: is incorrect. This is factually wrong — network policies can absolutely be scoped to individual users.

questions.stem: A company operating on AWS is implementing AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake. Select TWO accurate statements about this configuration. (Select TWO)

options.text: After enabling PrivateLink, all clients must use a new account URL that includes 'privatelink' in the hostname
options.explanation: is correct. When AWS PrivateLink is configured, clients must use the PrivateLink-specific account URL (e.g., account.privatelink.snowflakecomputing.com) — the standard public URL will not route through PrivateLink.

options.text: AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake is available on all Snowflake editions including Standard
options.explanation: is incorrect. PrivateLink is available only on Business Critical edition and higher — not on Standard.

options.text: Traffic between the VPC and Snowflake over PrivateLink traverses the public internet but is encrypted
options.explanation: is incorrect. The key benefit of PrivateLink is that traffic does NOT traverse the public internet — it stays on the AWS backbone network.

options.text: PrivateLink requires the Snowflake account and the VPC to be in the same AWS region
options.explanation: is correct. The Snowflake account and the AWS VPC must be in the same AWS region for PrivateLink connectivity.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is rotating key pair authentication credentials for a service account without causing any downtime. The service account currently has RSA_PUBLIC_KEY set. What is the correct rotation procedure?

options.text: ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>'; (This replaces the old key immediately)
options.explanation: is incorrect. Replacing RSA_PUBLIC_KEY directly causes immediate downtime — there is a window where neither key is valid during the transition.

options.text: ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 = '<new_key>'; (Update application to use new key, then run: ALTER USER svc_acct UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY; ALTER USER svc_acct SET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>'; UNSET RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2;)
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake supports two RSA public key slots (RSA_PUBLIC_KEY and RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2) specifically to enable zero-downtime key rotation. Set the new key in RSA_PUBLIC_KEY_2 while the old key remains active, update the application to use the new key, verify it works, then remove the old key.

options.text: DROP USER svc_acct; CREATE USER svc_acct RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>';
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the user loses all associated grants and role assignments.

options.text: ALTER USER svc_acct ROTATE RSA_PUBLIC_KEY = '<new_key>';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ROTATE is not a valid Snowflake ALTER USER keyword.

questions.stem: A warehouse has AUTO_SUSPEND set to 60 seconds and AUTO_RESUME = TRUE. A batch job submits queries every 5 minutes. What is the expected behavior?

options.text: The warehouse stays running continuously because queries arrive before the 60-second idle threshold expires
options.explanation: is incorrect. The 5-minute gap between batch runs far exceeds the 60-second auto-suspend, so the warehouse does not stay running.

options.text: AUTO_RESUME only works if a human manually resumes the warehouse first
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_RESUME works for any client connection, not just manual resumption.

options.text: The warehouse suspends between each batch run and auto-resumes for each query, charging for each resume cycle
options.explanation: is correct. With AUTO_SUSPEND = 60 seconds and queries arriving every 5 minutes, the warehouse will idle for 60 seconds after each batch completes, then suspend. When the next query arrives 5 minutes later (well past the 60-second threshold), AUTO_RESUME = TRUE triggers the warehouse to start automatically. Each resume incurs a minimum billing charge.

options.text: The warehouse will remain suspended after the first query completes because the batch job ends
options.explanation: is incorrect. The warehouse does not permanently suspend; it auto-resumes when the next query arrives.

questions.stem: A data science team runs very large Snowpark ML model training jobs that frequently run out of memory on a standard extra-large warehouse. What warehouse type should the administrator provision to address this?

options.text: A STANDARD warehouse with SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY to maximize memory across nodes
options.explanation: is incorrect. SCALING_POLICY = ECONOMY on a standard warehouse controls multi-cluster scaling behavior, not per-node memory.

options.text: A multi-cluster STANDARD warehouse with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4 to distribute the memory workload
options.explanation: is incorrect. Multi-cluster warehouses scale for concurrency (more simultaneous users/queries), not for per-query memory capacity — each cluster still has the same per-node memory.

options.text: A STANDARD warehouse with QUERY_ACCELERATION enabled to extend memory capacity on demand
options.explanation: is incorrect. Query Acceleration Service offloads parts of queries to serverless compute for large data scans but does not increase warehouse memory.

options.text: A SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse, which provides 16x more memory per node than a standard warehouse of the same size
options.explanation: is correct. SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouses provide 16x more memory per node compared to standard warehouses of the same size, making them ideal for Snowpark ML training jobs, pandas on Snowflake, and other memory-intensive workloads.

questions.stem: A DBA wants to understand the clustering efficiency of a large table before enabling automatic clustering. Which TWO commands can provide information about the table's current clustering state? (Select TWO)

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('mydb.myschema.orders', '(order_date, customer_id)');
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION returns a JSON object with detailed clustering metrics including average_depth, average_overlap, and partition statistics for the specified columns.

options.text: SHOW CLUSTER KEYS IN TABLE mydb.myschema.orders;
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW CLUSTER KEYS shows what clustering keys are defined on the table but does not provide depth or efficiency metrics.

options.text: SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('mydb.myschema.orders'));
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CLUSTERING_INFORMATION is not valid syntax — the function is a system function called directly, not an INFORMATION_SCHEMA table function.

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH('mydb.myschema.orders', '(order_date, customer_id)');
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_DEPTH returns just the average depth value as a scalar, useful for quick checks. Both accept optional column expressions.

questions.stem: A table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 set at the table level, but the database has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. A row is accidentally deleted. Can Time Travel recover this data?

options.text: Yes, because the database-level setting of 7 days overrides the table-level setting of 0
options.explanation: is incorrect. Higher-level (database) settings do NOT override more specific (table) settings — it works the opposite way.

options.text: Yes, but only within a 1-hour window because Snowflake has a minimum retention of 1 hour
options.explanation: is incorrect. Snowflake does NOT have a minimum 1-hour retention override; when retention = 0, there is no Time Travel history.

options.text: No, but Fail-Safe can recover data for up to 7 days after deletion even with retention set to 0
options.explanation: is partially true but misleading. For permanent tables on Enterprise edition and above, Fail-Safe does apply for 7 days even with retention = 0. However, Fail-Safe data is accessible only by Snowflake Support, not by the customer directly — so it cannot be used for self-service row recovery.

options.text: No, because the table-level setting of 0 takes precedence and disables Time Travel for this specific table
options.explanation: is correct. In Snowflake, the most specific (lowest) level setting takes precedence. A table-level DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 explicitly disables Time Travel for that table, overriding the database-level setting.

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to audit which users accessed a specific table containing PII data over the past 6 months. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides column-level access tracking with sufficient historical retention?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY shows query text and metadata but does not provide structured column-level access lineage.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. ACCESS_HISTORY provides table- and column-level access records showing which users accessed which objects (including reads and writes), making it ideal for PII access auditing. It retains data for 1 year.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.QUERY_HISTORY only retains 7 days of data — far too short for a 6-month audit.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY tracks authentication events, not data access.

questions.stem: A security audit requires a list of all active masking policies and the tables/columns they are currently applied to. Which approach provides this information most directly?

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES filtered by POLICY_KIND = 'MASKING_POLICY'
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES is a view showing all policy associations across the account — including which masking policies are applied to which table columns. Filtering by POLICY_KIND = 'MASKING_POLICY' gives a direct, account-wide list.

options.text: Run SHOW MASKING POLICIES and manually cross-reference with SHOW COLUMNS for each table
options.explanation: is incorrect. This approach is manual and error-prone for large accounts — SHOW MASKING POLICIES lists policies but not their column associations.

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.POLICY_REFERENCES() table function per schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.POLICY_REFERENCES() works per-database and has only 7-day retention; ACCOUNT_USAGE is more comprehensive for a full security audit.

options.text: Query SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY and filter for masking policy events
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY does not contain policy association metadata — it tracks data access events.

questions.stem: An administrator uses SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and sees a monitor with STATUS = 'SUSPENDED'. What does this indicate?

options.text: The resource monitor itself has been disabled by an administrator and is not tracking credits
options.explanation: is incorrect. A disabled resource monitor would show a different status — SUSPENDED refers to the warehouse state, not the monitor being disabled.

options.text: The quota period has ended and the monitor is waiting for the next reset cycle
options.explanation: is incorrect. End-of-period reset shows as the monitor resetting its counter, not a SUSPENDED status.

options.text: One or more warehouses assigned to this monitor have been suspended due to the monitor's trigger action
options.explanation: is correct. In SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS, a STATUS of SUSPENDED means the resource monitor triggered a SUSPEND or SUSPEND_IMMEDIATELY action and one or more warehouses were suspended because a credit threshold was reached. The resource monitor itself continues to function and track credits.

options.text: The monitor has detected a billing anomaly and suspended itself for safety
options.explanation: is incorrect. Resource monitors do not self-suspend; the SUSPENDED status reflects the warehouse suspension triggered by the monitor's configured action.

questions.stem: A user reports they cannot see data in a view that they previously had access to. The administrator runs SHOW GRANTS TO ROLE user_role and confirms the role has SELECT on the view. Select TWO additional checks the administrator should perform to diagnose the issue. (Select TWO)

options.text: Verify the user's role has USAGE privilege on the database and schema containing the view
options.explanation: is correct. If USAGE on the database or schema was recently revoked, the user can no longer navigate to the object even with SELECT on the view.

options.text: Check whether the view's underlying base tables have a row access policy that now excludes the user
options.explanation: is correct. A row access policy on the base tables may have changed, causing the view to return no rows (which looks like an access issue).

options.text: Confirm the user has not exceeded their session credit quota
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no session credit quota that affects query execution.

options.text: Run GRANT SELECT ON VIEW to refresh the privilege cache
options.explanation: is incorrect. Re-granting SELECT does not refresh a privilege cache — Snowflake evaluates privileges in real time and re-granting an existing privilege has no effect.

questions.stem: An administrator runs ALTER ACCOUNT SET LOCK_TIMEOUT = 3600. Which behavior does this parameter control?

options.text: It limits the total execution time of any SQL statement to 3600 seconds
options.explanation: is incorrect. Total statement execution time is controlled by STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS, not LOCK_TIMEOUT.

options.text: It sets how long (in seconds) a query will wait to acquire a table lock before returning an error
options.explanation: is correct. LOCK_TIMEOUT specifies the maximum number of seconds a SQL statement will wait to acquire a lock on a resource (such as a table lock during DML). If a lock cannot be obtained within this duration, the statement returns an error.

options.text: It defines the session idle timeout before a user is automatically logged out
options.explanation: is incorrect. Session idle behavior is controlled by session policies or UI settings, not LOCK_TIMEOUT.

options.text: It controls how long a suspended warehouse will remain in the suspended state
options.explanation: is incorrect. Warehouse suspension duration is not controlled by LOCK_TIMEOUT — warehouses auto-resume on demand.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to inspect the current value and scope of the LOCK_TIMEOUT parameter at the account level. Which command provides this?

options.text: DESCRIBE PARAMETER LOCK_TIMEOUT;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE PARAMETER is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS WHERE NAME = 'LOCK_TIMEOUT';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have an ACCOUNT_PARAMETERS view.

options.text: SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'LOCK%' IN ACCOUNT;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW PARAMETERS LIKE 'LOCK%' IN ACCOUNT lists all account-level parameters matching the pattern. The output shows the current value, the default, the level at which it was set, and its type.

options.text: SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS WHERE NAME = 'LOCK_TIMEOUT';
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW ACCOUNT PARAMETERS with a WHERE clause is not standard Snowflake syntax — the LIKE filter is the correct approach.

questions.stem: A user's account has been locked due to too many failed login attempts. The administrator needs to immediately unlock the account. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET DISABLED = FALSE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DISABLED = FALSE re-enables a manually disabled user account but does not clear a lockout state caused by failed login attempts — these are separate states.

options.text: GRANT LOGIN TO USER locked_user;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT LOGIN is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user RESET LOCKOUT;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER USER RESET LOCKOUT is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: ALTER USER locked_user SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0;
options.explanation: is correct. When a user is locked out due to failed login attempts, ALTER USER ... SET MINS_TO_UNLOCK = 0 immediately unlocks the account by setting the remaining lockout time to zero minutes.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is creating a new user for a business analyst. The analyst should automatically connect to the ANALYTICS_WH warehouse and use the ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC schema by default. Select TWO user properties that configure this login experience. (Select TWO)

options.text: DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH'
options.explanation: is correct. DEFAULT_WAREHOUSE sets the warehouse that is automatically activated when the user logs in.

options.text: DEFAULT_NAMESPACE = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC'
options.explanation: is correct. DEFAULT_NAMESPACE specifies the default database and schema (in the format DB.SCHEMA) so the user does not need to run USE DATABASE or USE SCHEMA on login.

options.text: AUTO_CONNECT_WAREHOUSE = 'ANALYTICS_WH'
options.explanation: is incorrect. AUTO_CONNECT_WAREHOUSE is not a valid Snowflake user property.

options.text: DEFAULT_SCHEMA = 'ANALYST_DB.PUBLIC'
options.explanation: is incorrect. DEFAULT_SCHEMA is not a valid Snowflake user property — the correct property for default schema is DEFAULT_NAMESPACE (which combines database and schema).

questions.stem: A governance team wants to add a description to custom roles to document their purpose. Which command adds a comment to a role named FINANCE_READ_ROLE?

options.text: ALTER ROLE finance_read_role SET DESCRIPTION = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIPTION is not a valid role property — Snowflake uses COMMENT for this purpose.

options.text: COMMENT ON ROLE finance_read_role IS 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
options.explanation: is incorrect. COMMENT ON ROLE follows a different SQL dialect not used in Snowflake.

options.text: ALTER ROLE finance_read_role SET COMMENT = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
options.explanation: is correct. The correct syntax to add documentation to a role is ALTER ROLE role_name SET COMMENT = '...'. The COMMENT property appears when running SHOW ROLES.

options.text: UPDATE ROLE finance_read_role COMMENT = 'Read-only access to finance schemas';
options.explanation: is incorrect. UPDATE ROLE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A stored procedure needs to determine the name of the Snowflake task that is currently executing it in order to log task context to an audit table. Which system function returns this information?

options.text: CURRENT_TASK()
options.explanation: is incorrect. CURRENT_TASK() is not a valid Snowflake function.

options.text: TASK_CONTEXT()
options.explanation: is incorrect. TASK_CONTEXT() is not a standard Snowflake function.

options.text: SYSTEM$TASK_NAME()
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSTEM$TASK_NAME() is not a standard Snowflake system function.

options.text: SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME()
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$CURRENT_USER_TASK_NAME() is the Snowflake system function that returns the fully-qualified name of the task currently executing a stored procedure or other code. This is useful for logging, auditing, and conditional logic based on which task triggered execution.

questions.stem: An administrator runs a script that creates roles as part of infrastructure provisioning. The script may be run multiple times. Which syntax safely creates a role without failing if it already exists?

options.text: CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS provisioned_role;
options.explanation: is correct. CREATE ROLE IF NOT EXISTS is the idempotent syntax — if the role already exists, the command succeeds silently without error and without modifying the existing role.

options.text: CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE provisioned_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE OR REPLACE ROLE would drop and recreate the role, losing all existing grants to and from that role, which is destructive in a provisioning scenario.

options.text: CREATE ROLE provisioned_role OR IGNORE;
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE ROLE ... OR IGNORE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: UPSERT ROLE provisioned_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. UPSERT ROLE is not a valid Snowflake command.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to find all roles whose names begin with 'ANALYST'. Which command returns this filtered list?

options.text: SELECT ROLE_NAME FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME LIKE 'ANALYST%';
options.explanation: is partially valid — the ACCOUNT_USAGE.ROLES view does exist and supports SQL filtering, but SHOW ROLES LIKE is the direct, real-time DDL command without the latency of ACCOUNT_USAGE views.

options.text: LIST ROLES WHERE NAME STARTS WITH 'ANALYST';
options.explanation: is incorrect. LIST ROLES WHERE NAME STARTS WITH is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: DESCRIBE ROLES FILTER 'ANALYST%';
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE ROLES FILTER is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: SHOW ROLES LIKE 'ANALYST%';
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW ROLES LIKE 'pattern' filters the role list using SQL LIKE syntax where % matches any sequence of characters. This is the direct DDL approach.

questions.stem: Snowflake periodically releases behavior change bundles that modify existing functionality. An administrator wants to test a specific bundle called '2024_01' in a development account before enabling it in production. Which approach is correct?

options.text: ALTER SESSION SET ENABLE_BCR_BUNDLES = '2024_01';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER SESSION SET ENABLE_BCR_BUNDLES is not a valid session parameter.

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE = '2024_01' IN ACCOUNT dev_account;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT SET BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE with IN ACCOUNT is not valid syntax.

options.text: CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TEST BUNDLE = '2024_01';
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREATE BEHAVIOR CHANGE TEST BUNDLE is not a Snowflake construct.

options.text: SELECT SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('2024_01');
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$ENABLE_BEHAVIOR_CHANGE_BUNDLE('bundle_name') is the system function used to enable a specific behavior change bundle in an account. This allows administrators to opt in to bundle changes in a dev/test account first before the bundle becomes mandatory.

questions.stem: An administrator runs SHOW RESOURCE MONITORS and sees one monitor showing CREDIT_QUOTA = 1000 and CREDITS_USED = 423. What does this tell the administrator?

options.text: The monitor has 577 credits remaining before any trigger actions fire
options.explanation: is correct. CREDITS_USED / CREDIT_QUOTA = 423 / 1000 = 42.3%, meaning the monitor has consumed 42.3% of its quota. If the first trigger is set at, say, 75%, no trigger has fired yet. There are 577 credits remaining before the quota is exhausted.

options.text: The monitor's triggers have already fired because 423 > 100 (the implicit 100% threshold)
options.explanation: is incorrect. Triggers fire at configured PERCENT thresholds (e.g., 75%, 100%), not at a fixed credit value of 100.

options.text: The 423 credits represent only serverless costs; warehouse costs are not reflected here
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREDITS_USED in a resource monitor tracks virtual warehouse credits — serverless credits are not tracked by resource monitors.

options.text: The CREDITS_USED will reset to 0 every 24 hours automatically
options.explanation: is incorrect. CREDITS_USED resets based on the monitor's FREQUENCY period (monthly, weekly, etc.), not daily.

questions.stem: A resource monitor has NOTIFY_USERS = ('ADMIN1', 'FINANCE_MGR') configured. When a trigger threshold is reached, who receives the notification email?

options.text: All ACCOUNTADMIN users in the account, plus ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR
options.explanation: is incorrect. When a NOTIFY_USERS list is specified, only those users are notified — not all ACCOUNTADMIN users. If NOTIFY_USERS is empty, all ACCOUNTADMIN users receive notifications by default.

options.text: The user who created the resource monitor, plus ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR
options.explanation: is incorrect. The creator of the resource monitor is not automatically notified unless they are in NOTIFY_USERS.

options.text: Only ADMIN1 and FINANCE_MGR, as specified in the NOTIFY_USERS list
options.explanation: is correct. The NOTIFY_USERS list specifies exactly which Snowflake users receive email notification when a NOTIFY trigger fires. Only the users named in that list receive the email. Users in NOTIFY_USERS must have a valid email address in their Snowflake user profile.

options.text: All users with ACCOUNTADMIN or SYSADMIN roles in the account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Notification goes only to the explicitly listed users, not all ACCOUNTADMIN/SYSADMIN holders.

questions.stem: Halfway through the month, a business unit requests an emergency increase to their warehouse's credit quota. The current monitor has CREDIT_QUOTA = 500. The administrator needs to raise it to 750 without resetting the usage counter. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR ... SET CREDIT_QUOTA = N changes the quota mid-period without resetting the CREDITS_USED counter. This preserves current-period tracking.

options.text: DROP RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor; CREATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor WITH CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
options.explanation: is incorrect. Dropping and recreating the monitor resets the usage counter and loses history — it would also briefly leave the warehouse unmonitored.

options.text: ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor ADD CREDIT_QUOTA = 250;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ADD CREDIT_QUOTA is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR dept_monitor SET CREDIT_QUOTA = 750;
options.explanation: is incorrect. UPDATE RESOURCE MONITOR is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A team is concerned about query queuing during peak hours on a shared warehouse. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view contains data about queued query wait times and warehouse load?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY focuses on credit usage, not load metrics or queue depth.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME > 0
options.explanation: is incorrect. While QUERY_HISTORY has a QUEUED_OVERLOAD_TIME column for per-query analysis, WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY provides the aggregate warehouse-level view needed for pattern analysis.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY tracks lifecycle events like starts and suspensions, not load or queuing metrics.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. WAREHOUSE_LOAD_HISTORY (also known as WAREHOUSE_LOAD_MONITORING) provides interval snapshots of warehouse load metrics including the number of queries running, queued, and blocked. It is specifically designed to identify periods of overload and queuing pressure.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to understand how storage consumption has changed month-over-month over the past year. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this historical storage data?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS
options.explanation: is incorrect. TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS gives a per-table breakdown but is a current-state view rather than a historical series.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. METERING_DAILY_HISTORY covers compute credits, not storage bytes.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. DATABASE_STORAGE_USAGE_HISTORY provides per-database historical data but is more granular and less suited for account-wide trending.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.STORAGE_USAGE provides a daily snapshot of total storage consumed by the account, including average bytes used for databases, internal stages, and Snowflake-managed stages. It retains up to 1 year of data, making it ideal for month-over-month trend analysis.

questions.stem: An administrator suspects Snowpipe and serverless task costs are unexpectedly high. Which TWO ACCOUNT_USAGE views should be consulted to identify the source of these serverless charges? (Select TWO)

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY — for serverless task credit consumption per task
options.explanation: is correct. SERVERLESS_TASK_HISTORY contains credit consumption records for each serverless task execution, allowing drill-down by task name, run time, and credits used.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY — for Snowpipe credit consumption per pipe
options.explanation: is correct. PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY records Snowpipe usage and credits consumed per pipe over time.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY — for all compute credit usage including Snowpipe
options.explanation: is incorrect. WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY only captures virtual warehouse credits — it does not include Snowpipe or serverless task credits.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.RESOURCE_MONITOR_HISTORY — for serverless resource monitor triggers
options.explanation: is incorrect. RESOURCE_MONITOR_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view for tracking serverless costs.

questions.stem: An administrator runs: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA sales TO ROLE reporting_role; A new table is created in the SALES schema the following week. Does REPORTING_ROLE automatically have SELECT on the new table?

options.text: Yes, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES applies to all current and future tables in the schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALL TABLES does not include future tables — that is the purpose of FUTURE TABLES syntax.

options.text: Yes, but only if the new table is created by the role that owns the schema
options.explanation: is incorrect. The creator of the new table is irrelevant; ALL TABLES is strictly a snapshot grant.

options.text: No, because GRANT ON ALL TABLES is a one-time bulk grant for tables that exist at execution time; future tables require a separate GRANT ON FUTURE TABLES
options.explanation: is correct. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA is a bulk point-in-time grant covering only tables that exist when the command executes. Tables created after this command are not covered. To handle future tables, a separate GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA must be issued.

options.text: No, because schema-level grants are not valid in Snowflake — grants must be made per table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Schema-level bulk grants are entirely valid in Snowflake.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to remove SELECT privilege on a specific table T from ROLE_R without affecting any other privileges ROLE_R has on other objects. Which command achieves this targeted revoke?

options.text: REVOKE ALL ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r;
options.explanation: is incorrect. REVOKE ALL removes all privileges on that table (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT, etc.) — not just SELECT.

options.text: REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r;
options.explanation: is correct. REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t FROM ROLE role_r removes only the SELECT privilege on that specific table. All other grants held by role_r on other objects remain intact.

options.text: ALTER ROLE role_r REMOVE PRIVILEGE SELECT ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ROLE REMOVE PRIVILEGE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON TABLE mydb.myschema.t TO ROLE sysadmin REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT OWNERSHIP REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS transfers ownership and revokes grants — it is not a targeted privilege revoke operation.

questions.stem: A stored procedure is created with EXECUTE AS OWNER. Inside the procedure, the developer uses IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('FINANCE_ROLE') to check if the calling user has a certain role. What does this function return in the caller's rights context?

options.text: It always returns TRUE because the procedure runs as the owner, who has all privileges
options.explanation: is incorrect. The function inspects the INVOKER's role, not the owner's privileges. It does not return TRUE for all callers.

options.text: It returns TRUE if the role that invoked the stored procedure has FINANCE_ROLE granted to it (directly or through hierarchy)
options.explanation: is correct. IS_GRANTED_TO_INVOKER_ROLE('role_name') is specifically designed for use inside EXECUTE AS OWNER stored procedures to check whether the calling user's active role hierarchy includes the specified role. It returns TRUE if the invoker's role chain includes FINANCE_ROLE — either as the primary role or through role hierarchy inheritance.

options.text: It returns FALSE because stored procedures cannot inspect the caller's role grants
options.explanation: is incorrect. The whole purpose of this function is to allow owner's rights procedures to inspect the invoker's roles.

options.text: It returns TRUE only if FINANCE_ROLE is the current primary role of the invoking user
options.explanation: is incorrect. The function checks the entire role hierarchy, not just the current primary role.

questions.stem: A security review flags a concern: a role has been granted OWNERSHIP on a production table. Why is OWNERSHIP privilege considered a higher security risk than SELECT or INSERT?

options.text: The OWNERSHIP privilege allows the holder to read all data in the table, bypassing row access policies
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP does not bypass row access policies — those apply to all roles including owners.

options.text: OWNERSHIP automatically grants ACCOUNTADMIN-level access to the entire account
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP on a table does not grant ACCOUNTADMIN-level access; it is scoped to that specific object.

options.text: OWNERSHIP is permanent and cannot be transferred back to the original owner
options.explanation: is incorrect. OWNERSHIP can be transferred with GRANT OWNERSHIP — it is not permanent.

options.text: The OWNERSHIP privilege allows the holder to re-grant any privilege on the owned object to any role, including itself
options.explanation: is correct. OWNERSHIP privilege grants full control over an object, including the ability to issue GRANT and REVOKE statements on that object. This privilege escalation risk means a compromised role with OWNERSHIP can give any other role any privilege on that object.

questions.stem: A Snowflake account has a database DB_SHARED that was shared from another account via a data share. A role CONSUMER_ROLE needs to query objects in this shared database. Which grant is required that differs from a normal local database?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT USAGE on a shared database is not the correct privilege for inbound data shares.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES is valid for local tables but not for shared databases.

options.text: GRANT IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
options.explanation: is correct. For databases created from an inbound data share, the privilege model is different. Instead of USAGE on the database plus individual object grants, you grant IMPORTED PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE to a role. This single grant gives the role access to all objects within the shared database as defined by the share.

options.text: GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE ON DATABASE db_shared TO ROLE consumer_role;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT REFERENCE_USAGE is for cross-database views and is unrelated to inbound data sharing.

questions.stem: An administrator needs to view all privileges currently granted on a specific table PROD.SALES.ORDERS to understand who has access. Which command provides this information?

options.text: SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE prod.sales.orders;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE prod.sales.orders lists all privilege grants on that specific table — including which roles have SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and OWNERSHIP — along with who granted each privilege and when. This is the most direct command for auditing access to a specific object.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES WHERE NAME = 'ORDERS';
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES can be filtered by table name but has a latency delay and is less direct for a real-time check.

options.text: DESCRIBE TABLE prod.sales.orders PRIVILEGES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE TABLE PRIVILEGES is not valid syntax.

options.text: SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES WHERE OBJECT_NAME = 'ORDERS';
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OBJECT_PRIVILEGES can provide this information but the view name and syntax differ — the correct INFORMATION_SCHEMA approach uses TABLE_PRIVILEGES.

questions.stem: A Snowflake Native App developer is building an application that uses Application Roles. Select TWO statements that are true about Application Roles in Snowflake Native Apps. (Select TWO)

options.text: Application Roles are defined within a Snowflake Native App and are granted to account roles in the consumer account
options.explanation: is correct. Application Roles are defined by the application provider within the app and represent access tiers (e.g., VIEWER, ADMIN) that consumers grant to their own account roles.

options.text: Application Roles can be granted directly to users in the consumer account without going through account roles
options.explanation: is incorrect. Application Roles are granted to account roles, not directly to users — they integrate into the standard role hierarchy.

options.text: Application Roles are visible in the consumer account via SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW APPLICATION ROLES IN APPLICATION app_name lists the available Application Roles for a specific installed app.

options.text: Application Roles automatically inherit all privileges of the SYSADMIN role in the consumer account
options.explanation: is incorrect. Application Roles have only the privileges the app provider defined; they do not automatically inherit SYSADMIN privileges.

questions.stem: An administrator has applied a network policy at the account level. A specific user VENDOR_ACCT needs access from an IP address that is not in the account policy's allowed list. What command assigns a user-level network policy to override the account policy for this user?

options.text: ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy FOR USER vendor_acct;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ALTER ACCOUNT with a FOR USER clause is not valid syntax.

options.text: GRANT NETWORK POLICY vendor_policy TO USER vendor_acct;
options.explanation: is incorrect. GRANT NETWORK POLICY is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: ALTER USER vendor_acct SET NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER USER user_name SET NETWORK_POLICY = policy_name assigns a network policy at the user level. A user-level network policy fully replaces the account-level policy for that specific user.

options.text: CREATE USER vendor_acct NETWORK_POLICY = vendor_policy;
options.explanation: is incorrect. While CREATE USER can set many properties, the standard approach for post-creation network policy assignment is ALTER USER, and NETWORK_POLICY is not a typical CREATE USER parameter.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to see all network policies defined in the account along with their allowed IP lists. Which command provides this?

options.text: SHOW NETWORK POLICIES;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW NETWORK POLICIES lists all network policies in the account, including the policy name, the ALLOWED_IP_LIST, the BLOCKED_IP_LIST, and assignment details. This is the standard DDL command for viewing policy configurations.

options.text: SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.NETWORK_POLICIES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCOUNT_USAGE does not have a NETWORK_POLICIES view.

options.text: DESCRIBE ALL NETWORK POLICIES;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE ALL NETWORK POLICIES is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: LIST NETWORK POLICIES IN ACCOUNT;
options.explanation: is incorrect. LIST NETWORK POLICIES is not valid Snowflake syntax.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is troubleshooting a failure where an external stage backed by an S3 bucket cannot be accessed. Which system function can help diagnose connectivity and permission issues with the storage integration used by the external stage?

options.text: SYSTEM$VALIDATE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION('my_s3_integration')
options.explanation: is correct. SYSTEM$VALIDATE_STORAGE_INTEGRATION validates the trust relationship and permissions between a Snowflake storage integration and the associated cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure). It returns diagnostic output indicating whether the IAM role/permissions are correctly configured.

options.text: SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('my_s3_stage')
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION is not a standard Snowflake system function.

options.text: SYSTEM$CHECK_STAGE_ACCESS('@my_s3_stage')
options.explanation: is incorrect. SYSTEM$CHECK_STAGE_ACCESS is not a valid Snowflake function.

options.text: SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGE_VALIDATION('my_s3_stage'));
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGE_VALIDATION is not a valid table function.

questions.stem: A company has enabled AWS PrivateLink for Snowflake and also has a network policy with ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('10.0.0.0/8'). A client connects via the PrivateLink endpoint from a VPC with private IP 10.5.0.25. Which statement accurately describes the interaction?

options.text: The connection is blocked because PrivateLink connections bypass the network policy IP check entirely, and only specific PrivateLink-enabled roles can connect
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no concept of PrivateLink-enabled roles — PrivateLink affects network routing, not role privileges.

options.text: The connection succeeds because 10.5.0.25 is within the 10.0.0.0/8 CIDR range in the network policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. Although 10.5.0.25 is in the 10.0.0.0/8 range, the network policy IP check doesn't apply to PrivateLink traffic as stated.

options.text: PrivateLink traffic bypasses IP-based network policy checks because traffic does not arrive from a public IP; the network policy is effectively ignored for PrivateLink connections
options.explanation: is correct. When clients connect via AWS PrivateLink, traffic arrives at Snowflake from the private AWS network without a routable public IP. Because IP-based network policies evaluate the source IP address and PrivateLink traffic does not present a standard public IP, the IP-based network policy is effectively bypassed. To enforce controls for PrivateLink traffic, administrators should use AWSVPCEID-type network rules.

options.text: The connection fails because PrivateLink connections must use a separate AWSVPCEID-type network rule, not an IP-based network policy
options.explanation: is incorrect. The connection does not automatically fail — PrivateLink traffic bypasses the IP policy rather than being blocked by it.

questions.stem: A security team wants to allow Snowflake connections only from a specific set of AWS VPC endpoints rather than relying on IP address ranges. Which network rule type should be used?

options.text: TYPE = IPV4 with VPC CIDR ranges
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = IPV4 uses IP address ranges — less precise and not VPC-endpoint-based.

options.text: TYPE = HOST with the VPC DNS names
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = HOST is for external function and egress domain rules, not for restricting inbound connections by VPC endpoint.

options.text: TYPE = AWSVPCEID with the VPC endpoint IDs as the value list
options.explanation: is correct. Snowflake's Network Rules support TYPE = AWSVPCEID, which accepts AWS VPC endpoint IDs (in the format vpce-xxxxxxxxx) in the VALUE_LIST. This restricts Snowflake access to connections arriving specifically through approved VPC endpoints.

options.text: TYPE = VPCENDPOINT with the endpoint service names
options.explanation: is incorrect. TYPE = VPCENDPOINT is not a valid network rule type in Snowflake.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to disable Time Travel on a large temporary staging table to reduce storage costs. The table is in a schema with DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7. Which command disables Time Travel specifically for this table?

options.text: ALTER TABLE staging_table SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
options.explanation: is correct. ALTER TABLE ... SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 disables Time Travel for that specific table without affecting the schema-level setting. The table-level setting overrides schema and account-level defaults.

options.text: ALTER SCHEMA staging_schema SET DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0;
options.explanation: is incorrect. Altering the schema would affect all tables in the schema, not just this one.

options.text: ALTER TABLE staging_table DISABLE TIME_TRAVEL;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DISABLE TIME_TRAVEL is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: DROP TABLE staging_table; CREATE TRANSIENT TABLE staging_table (...);
options.explanation: is incorrect. Creating a TRANSIENT TABLE also removes Time Travel (transient tables support only 0 or 1 day retention) but requires dropping and recreating the table, which is disruptive and potentially destructive.

questions.stem: A developer creates a TRANSIENT table for intermediate ETL processing. Compared to a permanent table and a TEMPORARY table, what are the key characteristics of a TRANSIENT table?

options.text: A transient table is session-scoped (dropped at session end) and has no Fail-Safe period, like a temporary table
options.explanation: is incorrect. Session-scoped behavior (dropped at session end) describes TEMPORARY tables, not transient tables.

options.text: A transient table persists across sessions, supports up to 90 days of Time Travel, and has a 7-day Fail-Safe period
options.explanation: is incorrect. Transient tables do not support up to 90 days of Time Travel and have no Fail-Safe — those are features of permanent Enterprise tables.

options.text: A transient table is dropped automatically after 24 hours and has no Time Travel or Fail-Safe
options.explanation: is incorrect. Transient tables do not automatically drop after 24 hours; they persist until explicitly dropped.

options.text: A transient table persists across sessions like a permanent table, supports 0 or 1 day of Time Travel retention, and has no Fail-Safe period
options.explanation: is correct. Transient tables persist across sessions and are visible to other users, like permanent tables. Unlike permanent tables, they support only 0 or 1 day of Time Travel (not up to 90 days) and have no Fail-Safe period. This makes them cheaper for non-critical intermediate data.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to check the Time Travel retention setting currently configured on several tables in a schema. Select TWO commands that display the retention_time column for tables. (Select TWO)

options.text: SHOW TABLES LIKE '%' IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema;
options.explanation: is correct. SHOW TABLES LIKE '%' IN SCHEMA includes a retention_time column in its output showing each table's configured DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS. It is fast for interactive use.

options.text: DESCRIBE DATABASE mydb;
options.explanation: is incorrect. DESCRIBE DATABASE describes the database object itself, not its tables.

options.text: SELECT TABLE_NAME, RETENTION_TIME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'MYSCHEMA';
options.explanation: is correct. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES also includes a RETENTION_TIME column that can be queried with SQL, enabling filtering and ordering across many tables.

options.text: SELECT TABLE_NAME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE COLUMN_NAME = 'RETENTION_TIME';
options.explanation: is incorrect. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS holds metadata about table columns (like data types), not table-level retention settings.

questions.stem: A team has a warehouse running mixed standard and Snowpark workloads. After profiling, they determine the Snowpark jobs need more memory but the standard SQL queries do not. What is the correct approach to address this without over-provisioning for standard queries?

options.text: Increase the warehouse size from XL to 2XL so all queries benefit from the additional memory
options.explanation: is incorrect. Increasing warehouse size improves performance for all workloads but does not address the memory efficiency gap — a 2XL STANDARD warehouse still has less memory per node than a SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse of the same size.

options.text: Create a separate SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse for Snowpark jobs and keep the existing standard warehouse for SQL queries
options.explanation: is correct. Route Snowpark jobs to a dedicated SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED warehouse (which provides 16x more memory per node) while keeping standard SQL queries on a regular STANDARD warehouse sized appropriately. This avoids overpaying for memory on workloads that don't need it.

options.text: Enable QUERY_ACCELERATION on the existing warehouse — it automatically adds memory for Snowpark workloads
options.explanation: is incorrect. Query Acceleration Service parallelizes large scans; it does not increase per-node memory for Snowpark.

options.text: Alter the existing warehouse: ALTER WAREHOUSE mixed_wh SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED;
options.explanation: is incorrect. While ALTER WAREHOUSE SET WAREHOUSE_TYPE = SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED is valid syntax, it converts the shared warehouse and impacts all workloads equally rather than separating them.

questions.stem: An administrator queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.TABLE_STORAGE_METRICS and sees a table with ACTIVE_BYTES = 5GB, TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES = 20GB, and FAILSAFE_BYTES = 15GB. Select TWO true statements about this scenario. (Select TWO)

options.text: The 20GB of TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES is billed at the same per-TB rate as the 5GB of active data
options.explanation: is correct. Time Travel storage is billed at the same per-TB rate as active storage.

options.text: The 15GB of FAILSAFE_BYTES is recoverable by the customer using UNDROP or Time Travel queries
options.explanation: is incorrect. Fail-Safe is Snowflake-managed recovery and is NOT accessible to customers via UNDROP or Time Travel — only Snowflake Support can access Fail-Safe data.

options.text: Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 on this table would immediately reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES to near zero
options.explanation: is incorrect. Reducing retention does not immediately purge Time Travel data; Snowflake's background processes clean up historical versions with a lag.

options.text: Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table will eventually reduce TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES as old historical versions age out
options.explanation: is correct. Reducing DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS causes old versions to expire over time, eventually lowering TIME_TRAVEL_BYTES. The reduction is not immediate — background processes handle cleanup.

questions.stem: A compliance team needs to verify all data that left the Snowflake account through COPY INTO external stages or UNLOAD operations over the past 30 days, for data egress cost and compliance auditing. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides this information?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'COPY'
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY captures query execution metadata but does not provide structured egress tracking with byte-level volume data.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY filtered by QUERY_TYPE = 'UNLOAD'
options.explanation: is incorrect. ACCESS_HISTORY tracks which objects were read, not data movement volumes or external stage transfers.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY
options.explanation: is incorrect. STAGE_USAGE_HISTORY is not a standard ACCOUNT_USAGE view.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.DATA_TRANSFER_HISTORY records all data transfer events including data transferred to external stages (egress), showing bytes transferred, transfer type, source and destination region, and timestamps. It is designed for both cost analysis and compliance verification of outbound data movement.

questions.stem: A security team wants to identify all active sessions and which client applications (e.g., JDBC driver version, Python connector) are currently connected to the Snowflake account. Which ACCOUNT_USAGE view provides both historical and current session information including client application details?

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.LOGIN_HISTORY — columns: CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID, CLIENT_APPLICATION_VERSION
options.explanation: is incorrect. LOGIN_HISTORY records login events but not the full session lifecycle or client environment details.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY — columns: SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_TYPE
options.explanation: is incorrect. QUERY_HISTORY has CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID but is scoped to individual queries rather than sessions.

options.text: SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS — columns: SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID, CREATED_ON, CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT
options.explanation: is correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.SESSIONS contains records for both active and historical sessions, including SESSION_ID, USER_NAME, CLIENT_APPLICATION_ID (the client application type and version), CREATED_ON, and CLIENT_ENVIRONMENT (platform details). This is the view designed for session auditing.

options.text: SHOW SESSIONS; — displays all currently active sessions with client application metadata
options.explanation: is incorrect. SHOW SESSIONS is not a standard Snowflake DDL command.

questions.stem: An organization wants to use a resource monitor to control costs at the organizational level across multiple Snowflake accounts. A Snowflake administrator proposes creating one resource monitor and assigning it to all accounts in the organization. Is this possible?

options.text: Yes, resource monitors can be created at the organization level using the ORGADMIN role and assigned to specific accounts
options.explanation: is incorrect. ORGADMIN provides organization-level visibility and billing data but cannot create cross-account resource monitors.

options.text: Yes, by creating the monitor in the organization master account, it automatically applies to all child accounts
options.explanation: is incorrect. There is no concept of an organization master account automatically propagating resource monitors to child accounts.

options.text: Yes, but only if all accounts are on the Business Critical edition
options.explanation: is incorrect. Edition requirements do not change the fundamental account-scoped nature of resource monitors.

options.text: No, resource monitors are account-scoped objects; each account requires its own resource monitor, and there is no organization-level resource monitor
options.explanation: is correct. Resource monitors are account-scoped objects in Snowflake. They can be assigned to specific warehouses within an account or to the account as a whole, but they cannot span multiple accounts. Each account in an organization must have its own resource monitors configured independently.
